content: In the circuit given below, the switch $S$ was kept open for a sufficiently long time and is closed at time $t = 0$. The time constant (in seconds) of the circuit for $t > 0$ is _______ .
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

At $t \geq \mathbf{0}$ (Transient) :

Switch is closed and circuit becomes as,

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { For R-L network time constant }(\tau)\\ &\tau=\frac{L}{R_{T H}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

where $R_{T H}=$ Thevenin resistance across L when all independent sources are deactivated.

$$ \begin{aligned} & R_{T H}=2 \Omega+(4 \Omega \| 4 \Omega)=2 \Omega+\left(\frac{4 \times 4}{8}\right) \\ & R_{T H}=2+2=4 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

From equation (i)

$$ \begin{aligned} \tau & =\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{sec} \\ \tau & =0.75 \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is $\mathbf{0 . 7 5}$.

content: As shown in the circuit, the initial voltage across the capacitor is 10 V, with the switch being open. The switch is then closed at $t = 0$. The total energy dissipated in the ideal Zener diode $(V_Z = 5 { V})$ after the switch is closed (in mJ, rounded off to three decimal places) is _________.
explanation:
Given :

(i) The initial voltage across the capacitor is 10 V .

(ii) $V_z=5 \mathrm{~V}$

Given circuit is shown below.

From the property of capacitor,

$$ V_C\left(0^{-}\right)=V_C\left(0^{+}\right)=10 \mathrm{~V} $$

Voltage across capacitor is given by.

$$ V_c(t)=V_c(\infty)+\left[V_c\left(0^{+}\right)-V_c(\infty)\right] e^{-t / \tau} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

(i) $\quad$ At $t \geq 0$ (Transient)

Switch $S$ is closed.

For RC Network.

Time constant,

$$ \begin{aligned} & (\tau)=R C \\ & \tau=\left(10 \times 10^3\right) \times\left(10 \times 10^{-6}\right) \\ & \tau=0.1 \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned} $$

Capacitors starts discharging through 10 k resistor and zener diode.

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_c(t)=V_0 e^{-t / \tau} \\ & V_c(t)=10 e^{-t / 0.1}=10 e^{-10 t} \\ & V_c(t)=10 e^{-10 t} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { For } t>0 \text {, } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & I_c(t)=\frac{(10-5)}{R} e^{-t / R C} \\ & I_c(t)=\frac{5}{R} e^{-t / R C} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} $$

Total Energy dissipated in zener diode is

$$ \begin{aligned} W & =\int_0^{\infty} P(t) d t=\int_0^{\infty}(5)\left(\frac{5}{R}\right) e^{-t / R C} d t \\ W & =\frac{25}{R} \int_0^{\infty} e^{-t / R C} d t=\frac{25}{R}(-R C)\left[e^{-t / R C}\right]_0^{\infty} \\ W & =(-25 C)\left[e^{-\infty}-e^{-0}\right] \\ W & =(-25 C)[0-1] \\ W & =25 C=25 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \\ W & =0.25 \mathrm{~mJ} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is $\mathbf{0 . 2 5 0}$.

content: In the circuit shown below, switch S was closed for a long time. If the switch is opened at t = 0, the maximum magnitude of the voltage $$\mathrm{V_R}$$, in volts, is __________ (rounded off to the nearest integer).
explanation:
Given circuit is

At $t=0^{-}$, switch is closed circuit is in steady state and inductor acts as S.C.

$$ i_L\left(0^{-}\right)=2 \mathrm{~A} $$

At $t=0^{+}$, switch is open and inductor acts as O.C.

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_R=2 \times-2=-4 V \\\\ & \text { Magnitude of } V_R=|-4| \\\\ & \left|V_R\right|=4 V \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is 4 .

content:
The circuit in the figure contains a current source driving a load having an inductor and a resistor in series, with a shunt capacitor across the load. The ammeter is assumed to have zero resistance. The switch is closed at time $t=0$.

Initially, when the switch is open, the capacitor is discharged, and the ammeter reads zero ampere. After the switch is closed, the ammeter reading keeps fluctuating for some time till it settles to a final steady value. The maximum ammeter reading that one will observe after the switch is closed (rounded off to two decimal places) is $\_\_\_\_$ A.
explanation:
From the mentioned data, we can say that all initial values = zero

For the condition $t>0$ : When switch is closed Redrawing the given circuit in $s$-domain, we get

From the mentioned data, we get

$$ V_c(s)=\frac{\frac{1}{s}(R+S L)}{L C S^2+R C S+1} $$

And$$\,\, I(S)=\frac{V_c(S)}{R+S L}=\frac{1}{S\left[L C S^2+R C S+1\right]} $$

or,$$\,\, \zeta=\frac{R}{2} \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}=\frac{5 K}{2} \sqrt{\frac{100 P}{10 m}} $$

We get,$$ \,\,\zeta=2.5 \times 0.1=0.25 $$

Also, % peak overshoot $=e^{-\pi \zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100$

or,$$\,\, =\frac{C t_{\max }-C(t)}{C(t)} \times 100=44 $$

or,$$ \,\,C(t)_{\max }-C(t)=0.44 C(t) $$

But$$\,\, C(t)=i(t) $$

$$ \begin{aligned}Then,\,\, I(t)_{\max }-1 & =0.44 \\ I(t)_{\max } & =1.44 \mathrm{Amp} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is ( 1.42 to 1.45 )

content:
The switch in the circuit in the figure is in position $P$ for a long time and then moved to position $Q$ at time $t=0$

The value of $\frac{d v(t)}{d t}$ at $t=0^{+}$is
explanation:
From above we have

$$ \frac{d V_c}{d t} \quad \text { at } \quad t=0^{+} $$

Now For condition when we have $t<0$ : And when Switch is connected at $P$ position

For $t=0$ : Circuit goes into steady state

From above we have $i_L\left(0^{+}\right)=\frac{20}{20 \mathrm{~K}}=1 \mathrm{~mA}$

$$ \begin{aligned}Also,  V_c(0) & =10 \mathrm{~K} \times 10 \mathrm{~mA} \\ & =10 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Now for the condition when $t>0$ : When switch is connected at $Q$ position

So for $t=0^{+}$

From above we have $\quad i_c\left(0^{+}\right)+2 \mathrm{~mA}+1 \mathrm{~mA}=0$

Also,  $$ i_c\left(0^{+}\right)=-3 \mathrm{~mA} $$

Then  $$ i_c=C \frac{d V_c}{d t} $$

Also  $$ \frac{d V_c}{d t}=\frac{i_c\left(0^{+}\right)}{c} \text { at } t=0^{+} $$

or, $$ =\frac{-3}{1} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{sec} $$

or, we have  $$ \frac{d V_c\left(0^{+}\right)}{d t}=-3 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{sec} $$

For the circuit shown below different time constants are given :

  1. 1. $0.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{sec}$

  2. 2. $2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{sec}$

  3. 3. $0.25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{sec}$

  4. 4. $10^{-3} \mathrm{sec}$

What are the charging and discharging times respectively :
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

(i) Calculation of charging time constant When switch is at position $A$,

Above figure is charging circuit, hence for a $R-C$ network, charging time constant is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tau_1=R_1 C_1=1 \times 10^3 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \\ & \tau_1=0.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned} $$

(ii) Calculation of discharging time constant :

When switch is at position $B$,

<

Above figure is a discharging circuit, because it does not have any independent source.

From figure, $C_2$ and $C_3$ are in series.

$$ C=\frac{C_2 C_3}{C_2+C_3}=\frac{0.5 \times 0.5}{0.5+0.5}=0.25 \mu \mathrm{~F} $$

For a $R$ - $C$ network, discharging time constant is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tau_2=R_2 C=10^3 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-6} \\ & \tau_2=0.25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (C).

content: The switch S$$_1$$ was closed and S$$_2$$ was open for a long time. At t = 0, switch S$$_1$$ is opened and S$$_2$$ is closed, simultaneously. The value of i$$_\mathrm{c}$$(0$$^+$$), in amperes, is
explanation:
Given circuit is,

At $t=0^{-}, S_1$ is closed and $S_2$ is opened, at steady state $L \rightarrow$ S.C., $C \rightarrow$ O.C.

$$ \begin{aligned} & i_L\left(0^{-}\right)=\frac{1 \times 25}{100+25}(\text { C.D.R }) \\\\ & i_L\left(0^{-}\right)=0.2 \mathrm{~A} \\\\ & V_C\left(0^{-}\right)=100 \times 0.2=20 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

At $t=0^{+}, S_1$ opened $S_2$ is closed $L \rightarrow$ O.C., $C \rightarrow$ S.C.

Applying KCL at node $A$,

$$ \begin{aligned} & i_C\left(0^{+}\right)+0.2+\frac{20}{25}=0 \\\\ & i_C\left(0^{+}\right)=-0.8-0.2 \\\\ & i_C\left(0^{+}\right)=-1 \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content:
In the circuit shown in the figure, the switch is closed at time $t=0$, while the capacitor is initially charges to -5 V (i.e., $\left(\theta_C(0)=-5 \mathrm{~V}\right)$.

The time after which the voltage across the capacitor becomes zero (rounded off to three decimal places) is $\_\_\_\_$ ms .
explanation:
From the mentioned data, we have

For  $$ V_c(0)=-5 \mathrm{~V} $$

Switch is closed for $t=0$

Now for $V_c(t)=0 \Rightarrow t=$ ?

For the situation when $t>0$ : when switch is closed

For $t=0^{+} V_c\left(0^{+}\right)=V_c(0)=-5 \mathrm{~V}$

For $t \rightarrow \infty$; circuit tends to is in steady state

From, above figure we have

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_c-5}{250}+\frac{V_R}{500}+\frac{V_c}{250}=0 \\or, & \quad 2 V_c-10+V_R+2 V_c=0 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{lc} \text { But } & 5-V_R-V_c=0 \\ \text { We have } & V_R=5-V_c \\ \text { or, } & 4 V_c-10+5-V_c=0 \\ \text { and, } & V_c=V_c(\infty)=\frac{5}{3} \mathrm{~V} \end{array} $$

Also, we have $\quad V_c(t)=V_c(\infty)+\left\{V_c\left(0^{+}\right)-V_c(\infty)\right\} e^{-t / T}$

$$ \tau=R_{e q} \cdot C_{e q} $$

$R_{e q}$ or $R_{\mathrm{th}}$ : Redrawing the given circuit for determing $R_{\mathrm{th}}$ value

we have  $$ R_{\mathrm{th}}=\frac{V_t}{I_t} \Omega $$

But we have  $$ V_R=-V t $$

Also we have,  $$ -I_t+\frac{V_t}{250}+\frac{V_R}{500}+\frac{V_t}{250}=0 $$

or,  $$ I_t=\frac{V_t}{250}-\frac{V_t}{500}+\frac{V_t}{250} $$

or,  $$ I_t=V_t \times \frac{3}{500} $$

Also we have, $\frac{V_t}{I_t}=R_{e q}=R_{\mathrm{th}}=\frac{500}{3} \Omega$

and,  $$ \tau=\frac{500}{3} \times 0.6 \times 10^{-6}=0.1 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{sec} $$

On putting the values,

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_c(t)=\frac{5}{3}+\left\{-5-\frac{5}{3}\right\}^{e^{-t / \tau}} \\ & V_c(t)=0 \text { and } t=? \end{aligned} $$

we have    $$ 0=\frac{5}{3}-\frac{20}{3} e^{-t / \tau} $$

or,    $$ \frac{1}{4}=e^{-t / \tau} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}or,    \ln \{0.25\} & =-t / \tau \\ -1.386 & =\frac{-t}{\tau} \\ t & =1.386 \tau \\ & =1.386 \times 0.1 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{sec} \\ t & =0.1386 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{sec} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is ( 0.13 to 0.14 ).

The voltage VC1, VC2 and VC3 across the capacitors in the circuit in Fig., under steady state, are respectively
For the compensated attenuator of figure, the impulse response under the condition $$R_1C_1\;=\;R_2C_2$$ is:

options.content: $$\frac{{\mathrm R}_2}{{\mathrm R}_1\;+\;{\mathrm R}_2}\left[1\;-\;\mathrm e^\frac1{{\mathrm R}_1{\mathrm C}_1}\right]\mathrm u\left(\mathrm t\right)$$
$$\frac{{\mathrm R}_2}{{\mathrm R}_1\;+\;{\mathrm R}_2}\mathrm e^\frac1{{\mathrm R}_1{\mathrm C}_1}\mathrm u\left(\mathrm t\right)$$

explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown below, } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & Z_1(s)=\frac{R_1 \times \frac{1}{C_1 s}}{R_1+\frac{1}{C_1 s}} \\ & Z_1(s)=\frac{R_1}{R_1 C_1 s+1}=\frac{R_1}{R_2 C_2 s+1} \end{aligned}\\ &\text { [Given : } R_1 C_1=R_2 C_2 \text { ] }\\ &Z_2(s)=\frac{R_2 \times \frac{1}{C_2 s}}{R_2+\frac{1}{C_2 s}}=\frac{R_2}{R_2 C_2 s+1} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V_2(s)}{V_1(s)}=\frac{Z_2(s)}{Z_1(s)+Z_2(s)} \quad[\mathrm{By} \mathrm{VDR}] \\ & \frac{V_2(s)}{V_1(s)}=\frac{\frac{R_2}{R_2 C_2 s+1}}{\frac{R_1}{R_2 C_2 s+1}+\frac{R_2}{R_2 C_2 s+1}}=\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \\ & V_2(s)=\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} V_1(s) \end{aligned} $$

Taking inverse Laplace transform,

$$ \begin{aligned} & v_2(t)=\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} v_1(t) \\ & v_1(t)=\delta(t)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text { [Given] } \\ & v_2(t)=\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \delta(t) \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: If the Laplace transform of the voltage across a capacitor of value of $$\frac12\;\mathrm F$$ is $$V_C\;\left(s\right)\;=\;\frac{s\;+\;1}{s^3\;+\;s^2\;+\;s\;+\;1}$$ , the value of the current through the capacitor at t = 0+ is
explanation:
Given : $C=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~F}, V_C(s)=\frac{s+1}{s^3+s^2+s+1}$

For capacitor, $X_C=\frac{1}{C s}=\frac{1}{\frac{1}{2} s}=\frac{2}{s}$

Transform voltage of capacitor is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_C(s)=I_C(s) X_C \\ & I_C(s)=\frac{V_C(s)}{X_C}=\frac{(s+1) s}{\left(s^3+s^2+s+1\right) 2} \end{aligned} $$

The value of current at $t=0^{+}$, means it is the initial value.

By initial value theorem,

$$ \begin{aligned} & i_C\left(0^{+}\right)=\lim _{s \rightarrow \infty} s I_C(s) \\ & i_C\left(0^{+}\right)=\lim _{s \rightarrow \infty} \frac{s \times s(s+1)}{2\left(s^3+s^2+s+1\right)} \\ & i_C\left(0^{+}\right)=\lim _{s \rightarrow \infty} \frac{s^2(s+1)}{2\left(s^2+1\right)(s+1)} \\ & i_C\left(0^{+}\right)=\lim _{s \rightarrow \infty} \frac{s^3\left(1+\frac{1}{s}\right)}{2 s^2\left(1+\frac{1}{s^2}\right) s\left(1+\frac{1}{s}\right)} \\ & i_C\left(0^{+}\right)=\frac{1}{2+0}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (C).

content: A 10 Ω resistor, a 1 H inductor and 1 $$\mathrm{μF}$$ capacitor are connected in parallel.The combination is driven by a unit step current.Under the steady state condition, the source current flows through
explanation:
$$ \text { According to the question, } $$

Under steady state condition, inductor behaves as a short circuit and capacitor behaves as an open circuit.

Hence, the source current flows through the inductor only.

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: A square waveform as shown in the figure is applied across 1 mH ideal inductor.The current through the inductor is a triangular wave of ................. peak amplitude.

options.content: triangular wave with peak amplitude of 0.5 Amp.
triangular wave with peak amplitude of 1.0 Amp.
triangular wave with peak amplitude of 2.0 Amp.
sine wave with peak amplitude of 0.5 Amp.

explanation:
Current through an inductor is given by,

$${i_L}(t) = {1 \over L}\int\limits_0^t {V(t)} dt$$

So, current through inductor is the integration of the applied voltage across the inductor.

Integration of square wave is a triangular wave.

The square wave can be written as,

$$V(t) = u(t) - 2u(t - 0.5) + 2u(t - 1) + ......$$

Integrating the voltage we get,

$${i_L}(t) = r(t) - 2r(t - 0.5) + 2r(t - 1) - ......$$


Ip = 0.5 Amp.

content:
